target.title: Add sessions_yield completion truth metadata
target.body:
## Summary

This patch adds an internal completion truth seam for `sessions_yield` and wires it through the embedded runner so parent/session orchestration can observe an explicit, trustworthy completion source.

For a real `sessions_yield` turn, the expected selected source is:

```ts
result.meta.completion.truthSelection.source === "toolResult"
```

## What changed

- Added internal `src/agents/completion-truth/` module:
  - source-priority selector
  - public-host/runtime queues
  - sessions_yield envelope builder
  - transcript-result normalizer/selector
- Added an internal `onCompletionTruth` callback to `sessions_yield` tooling.
- Wired embedded runner yield-abort handling to resolve completion truth from the explicit tool result, with low-confidence realtime-hint fallback.
- Added `meta.completion.truth` and `meta.completion.truthSelection` on run result completion meta.
- Redacted raw internal `sessionId` / `toolCallId` before truth metadata leaves the runner.
- Kept completion truth off plugin SDK / public package exports.
- Kept public `createOpenClawCodingTools` options free of `onCompletionTruth`; runner uses internal `createOpenClawCodingToolsInternal`.

## Safety / boundaries

- `onCompletionTruth` is best-effort observability and cannot block the `onYield` control path.
- Both async rejection and sync throw from the observer are covered.
- Completion truth resolver failure records `truthSelection: none` and still allows clean yield completion.
- No npm-global runtime patch.
- No plugin SDK export.
- No transcript polling expansion.
- No artifact auto-discovery.
- No source merge/speculative reconciliation.

## Tests / validation

Initial pre-rebase local validation on the original source baseline:

```text
git diff --check: passed
targeted oxlint: 0 warnings / 0 errors
focused tests: 6 files / 26 tests passed
full typecheck: passed
plugin SDK leakage grep: no internal completion-truth leakage
secret scan: no suspicious real key/token patterns
```

Post-rebase validation against current `main`:

```text
git diff --check: passed
focused tests: 9 files / 40 tests passed
```

Focused test command:

```bash
npx vitest run \
  src/agents/completion-truth.export.test.ts \
  src/agents/completion-truth/*.test.ts \
  src/agents/tools/sessions-yield-tool.test.ts \
  src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/sessions-yield.orchestration.test.ts \
  --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts
```

Gated live smoke was also validated earlier with `nowcoding/gpt-5.5`:

```text
src/agents/pi-embedded-runner.sessions-yield-completion-truth.live.test.ts
result.meta.completion.truthSelection.source === "toolResult"
```

### Post-rebase local typecheck note

After rebasing onto current `main`, full local typecheck currently reports errors outside this PR's changed file set, including `extensions/acpx/src/claude-agent-acp-completion.test.ts` missing `@agentclientprotocol/claude-agent-acp` and existing casts in `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.test.ts`. The PR-specific focused tests pass after conflict resolution.

## Reviewer notes

A deterministic non-live fake-provider/stream full-path test is not included in this patch. A first e2e attempt was withdrawn because current provider routing / harness did not reliably hit the mocked stream. The real path is covered by the gated live smoke. If default-CI full-path coverage is required, the next step should be a dedicated fake provider stream harness.

## Commit

```text
dee59cf6 Add sessions_yield completion truth metadata
```

target.title: fix: recover terminal session status on visible inbound turns
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #86827 — group chat sessions stuck in `failed` status silently drop all subsequent messages.

When a group chat session enters a terminal status (`failed`, `timeout`, or `killed`), the reply dispatch path returns a successful no-op (`queuedFinal: false`, zero dispatch counts) without starting an AI turn or delivering any user-visible error. All subsequent messages are silently discarded until a manual `/new` reset.

## Root Cause

Three independent mechanisms conspire to create the silent drop:

1. **Session state persistence**: `initSessionState()` reuses the existing session entry with its terminal `status` field intact, passing it downstream unchanged.
2. **Reply operation locking**: A stale `ReplyOperation` from the failed run remains registered in `replyRunRegistry`, causing `admitReplyTurn()` to return `skipped` with reason `active-run`.
3. **No fallback or diagnostic**: The `skipped` admission produces a valid dispatch result with zero counts, and no warning is logged — the message simply vanishes.

## Changes

### 1. Auto-recover terminal sessions (`session.ts`)

When a **visible** (non-cron/heartbeat) inbound message hits a session with recoverable terminal status, automatically clear the stale lifecycle fields (`status`, `startedAt`, `endedAt`, `runtimeMs`, `abortedLastRun`) while **preserving the `sessionId` and transcript continuity** — no session rotation.

### 2. Clear stale reply operations with race guard (`dispatch-from-config.ts`)

When `admitReplyTurn()` returns `skipped` with reason `active-run` but the persisted session is in terminal status, force-clear the stale reply operation via `forceClearReplyRunBySessionId()` and retry admission **once**. This breaks the deadlock without risking infinite retry loops.

**Concurrent recovery guard**: After a recovery turn clears the proven stale leftover and admits its own fresh operation, it is marked with a `terminalRecovery` flag. If a second visible message arrives against the same terminal snapshot before the first recovery clears the store state, the force-clear branch now skips operations marked as in-flight terminal recoveries. This prevents a sibling recovery turn from killing a fresh recovery that is already in flight, letting concurrent turns fall through to normal busy/wait handling instead.

### 3. Agent API path (`agent.ts`)

Mirror the same terminal recovery logic in the agent API dispatch path so agent API requests also clear terminal state for visible requests.

### 4. Diagnostics (`kernel.ts`, `types.ts`)

Add a structured warning log when a visible channel turn dispatch completes with `queuedFinal: false` and zero tool/block/final counts — a sentinel for future silent message drops. Excludes system turns (heartbeat/cron/exec) and observe-only admissions.

## Real behavior proof

Captured by driving the **real** runtime modules (`dispatchReplyFromConfig`, `initSessionState`, `reply-run-registry`) with verbose logging on, **no vitest and no module mocks** — only the production parameters the Gateway supplies plus a real on-disk JSON session store. A before/after contrast was produced by running the identical driver in a pre-fix worktree.

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A group-chat session left in a terminal lifecycle status (`failed`/`timeout`/`killed`) silently drops every later message — the stale lifecycle entry plus a leftover "active" reply operation block turn admission, so a subsequent visible inbound message gets no AI turn and no reply (#86827).
- **Real environment tested**: Compiled branch source at HEAD `ac52eb0b24`; macOS 15.3.1 (Darwin 24.3.0, arm64), Node `v24.10.0`. Product built with `pnpm build:docker` (exit 0); the recovery log string is present in compiled `dist/`. Evidence captured via `tsx` driving the real in-process dispatch/session/registry path. Before/after contrast run at pre-fix commit `8de37e1ce4` (parent of the first PR commit).
- **Failing proof before fix** (`8de37e1ce4`, identical driver + input): real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible group turn on a `status="failed"` session with a stale running reply op —
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  [dispatch] dispatch TIMED-OUT (no reply within 8s): visible turn stuck behind the stale terminal reply op -> SILENT DROP
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = null
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = true
  ```
  And real `initSessionState()` against an on-disk store: `RESULT: STILL-TERMINAL — session entry remains status=failed after a visible inbound turn`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm build:docker
  export OPENCLAW_HOME=$(mktemp -d) OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 \
         OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_PROVIDERS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_CRON=1
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts   # drives real dispatchReplyFromConfig()
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_proof_driver.mts      # drives real initSessionState() + registry
  ```
  The driver seeds a real session store with a terminal `failed` group entry, registers a real stale running reply operation, then calls the real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible inbound group message.
- **Evidence after fix (`ac52eb0b24`)**: copied live console output from running the real in-process driver `node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts` (drives the production `dispatchReplyFromConfig()`); redacted runtime log:
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  dispatch-from-config: cleared stale active reply operation for terminal session agent:main:telegram:group:<redacted>
  [dispatch] dispatch returned: {"queuedFinal":true,"counts":{"tool":0,"block":0,"final":1}}
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = {"kind":"failed","code":"run_failed"}
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = false
  ```
  Real session lifecycle: `initSessionState -> isNewSession=false sessionId=<same> abortedLastRun=false`, and `AFTER turn -> status=undefined ... RESULT: RECOVERED — terminal lifecycle cleared, same sessionId reused (transcript preserved)`.
- **Observed result after fix**: A visible inbound message to a terminal session (1) emits the new recovery log, (2) force-fails the stale leftover op and frees the registry (`isActive=false`), (3) produces a real queued reply (`queuedFinal:true, counts.final:1`) instead of the pre-fix zero-count silent drop / indefinite block, and (4) clears the persisted terminal lifecycle fields while reusing the same `sessionId` (transcript preserved). The pre-fix checkout, given identical input, leaves the session `failed` and the turn stuck/dropped.
- **What was not tested**: The full Docker image build failed at the `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` layer due to transient npm-registry CDN errors fetching optional native packages (network, not OOM and not code), so no end-to-end live channel socket round-trip was performed; the proof drives the real in-process dispatch/session/registry runtime that a channel turn funnels into. The `agent.ts` Gateway RPC path and the `kernel.ts` zero-count warning were source-verified but not separately runtime-exercised here. Concurrency races (sibling visible-turn recovery, heartbeat non-clobber) are covered by the added unit tests. Architecture: arm64 (macOS) only; CI truth is Linux x64 Node 24.

## Tests

- **session.test.ts**: Verify failed group sessions recover without rotating the transcript
- **dispatch-from-config.test.ts**: Verify stale active reply operations are cleared for terminal sessions and admission is retried; verify two concurrent visible turns against the same terminal snapshot do not kill each other's recovery operations
- **agent.test.ts**: Verify terminal failed agent API sessions recover without session rotation; updated existing failed-session tests to verify terminal state clearing
- **kernel.test.ts**: Verify zero-count visible dispatch warning is emitted
- **agent-runner-execution.test.ts / agent-runner-memory.test.ts**: Updated mock `ReplyOperation` stubs with new `terminalRecovery` field and `markTerminalRecovery` method

All focused tests pass across 2 Vitest shards (512 cases).

target.title: fix(memory): backfill provider.model in createWithAdapter when adapter returns empty string
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When a memory embedding provider adapter returns an empty model string,
the search index identity is overwritten with `""`, leaving the index
permanently dirty. This happens during boot when the provider
registration races with configuration loading.

## Why This Change Was Made

`createWithAdapter` passes the resolved model to the adapter but does not
inspect the returned `result.provider.model`. If the adapter returns an
empty string, downstream callers see `""` instead of the resolved model
name, which corrupts index identity comparison and prevents search from
working correctly.

The fix captures the resolved model before the adapter call and backfills
`result.provider.model` with it when the adapter returns an empty string.

## Changes

- **`extensions/memory-core/src/memory/embeddings.ts`** — store
  `resolvedModel` and backfill `result.provider.model` when empty
- **`extensions/memory-core/src/memory/embeddings.test.ts`** — add
  tests for the backfill case (adapter returns empty) and the
  already-populated case (adapter returns correct model)

## Evidence

- Unit tests cover both the backfill path (adapter returns `""`) and
  the preserve path (adapter returns the correct model)
- The empty-string check (`||`) correctly handles both `""` and `undefined`

## Related

Closes #90042
参考 #91660 — 相同修复思路，本 PR 含完整 Real behavior proof 和 CI 格式

## Summary
- add a canonical WhatsApp-local account policy seam in `extensions/whatsapp/src/account-policy.ts`
- migrate inbound policy, send, heartbeat recipient resolution, and action target auth to consume that seam instead of re-deriving multi-account policy locally
- add focused account-policy coverage and keep the existing WhatsApp consumer tests pinned to preserved behavior

## Preserved behavior
- `defaultAccount` selection and omitted-account fallback behavior
- `accounts.default` inheritance rules, including non-inheritance of default-only `authDir` and `selfChatMode`
- direct-chat outbound authorization and group-JID handling
- same-phone / self-chat handling
- pairing-store read behavior in allowlist vs pairing modes
- heartbeat recipient selection semantics
- action authorization account resolution

## Tests
- `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/account-policy.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/access-control.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/heartbeat-recipients.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/action-runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/send.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/accounts.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Notes
- keeps the PR focused on the WhatsApp plugin and directly related tests
- future changes to account selection, allowlists, DM/group defaults, and direct-target auth should now be mostly isolated to the new seam

target.title: fix(msteams): drop unsupported $search on msteams:search (AI-assisted)
target.body: ## Summary - **Problem:** `msteams:search` action hits `HTTP 400: Search is not supported` because Graph API blocks `$search` on `/chats/{id}/messages` and `/teams/*/channels/*/messages` when called with Application permissions (the default for bot auth). - **Why it matters:** The action is unusable for any bot running on app-only credentials — which is the most common msteams deployment. - **What changed:** Replace `$search` with a list-and-local-filter path that works under Application permissions. Sender filtering still pushes down via `$filter` (which Graph supports app-only). Local filter strips HTML bodies so queries match rendered text. - **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Delegated-auth code paths, other msteams actions, Graph token resolution, or `/search/query` callers elsewhere in the repo. Search range is now bounded to the recent list window rather than the full archive — full-archive search requires Delegated auth and is out of scope for this fix. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #70127 - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause - **Root cause:** The original implementation of `searchMessagesMSTeams` used `$search` in the Graph query string. Graph API documentation is explicit that `$search` on message collections is not available for Application permissions — only `/search/query` with Delegated auth supports it for chat messages, and even that blocks the `chatMessage` entity for app-only. - **Missing detection / guardrail:** No integration test hit a Graph-like 400 for `$search`. Unit tests mocked successful responses, so the permission mismatch never surfaced in CI. - **Contributing context:** The original PR (#54832, merged) assumed `$search` worked because it does on some other Graph collections under app-only. ## Regression Test Plan - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - **Target test or file:** `extensions/msteams/src/graph-messages.search.test.ts` - **Scenario the test should lock in:** The outgoing path must never contain `$search`, regardless of query value. New test `does not use Graph $search (unsupported under Application permissions)` asserts this directly. - **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The failure mode is a URL-shape contract with Graph. Locking the path-building logic at the unit seam catches any regression to `$search` without needing a live Graph call. - **Existing test that already covers this (if any):** None — prior tests only verified happy-path response parsing. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - `msteams:search` now works on app-only (bot) credentials instead of returning Graph 400. - Search range is now the most recent N messages (clamped 50–200 depending on requested limit) rather than the full conversation archive. This is a reduction in coverage for the (previously broken) Delegated-auth case, but the action was not functional under app-only at all before. - Local filter treats message bodies as HTML by default (stripping tags before matching), and only skips the strip for `contentType: "text"`. ## Diagram ```text Before (broken for app-only): search(query="hi") -> GET /chats/{id}/messages?$search="hi" -> HTTP 400: Search not supported After: search(query="hi") -> GET /chats/{id}/messages?$top=50 (+ optional $filter) -> stripHtml(body) when contentType=html -> local includes("hi") -> return up to limit ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? **No** — uses the same Graph scopes already required for the action. - Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No** - New/changed network calls? **No new endpoints.** Same `/chats/{id}/messages` and `/teams/*/channels/*/messages` endpoints, different query string. - Command/tool execution surface changed? **No** - Data access scope changed? **No** — identical data returned, just filtered locally instead of server-side. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - **OS:** macOS 24.5.0 (Darwin) - **Runtime/container:** Node v25.9.0, pnpm - **Model/provider:** N/A (API-path fix, no model interaction) - **Integration/channel:** msteams bundled plugin, Application permissions (app-only bot token) - **Relevant config:** N/A ### Steps 1. Configure an msteams bot with app-only (Application-permission) credentials. 2. Send messages to a chat where the bot is a member. 3. Invoke the `msteams:search` action against that chat with any query. ### Expected - Matching messages returned. ### Actual (before this PR) - Graph returned `HTTP 400: Search is not supported on this organization` regardless of query. ### Actual (after this PR) - Matching messages returned. Reproduced via unit tests covering $search removal, HTML stripping, `$filter` pushdown for sender, pagination window sizing, and `user:` target resolution. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after: `extensions/msteams/src/graph-messages.search.test.ts` now has 17 assertions locking the new contract; one specifically asserts the path never contains `$search`. - Targeted run: `pnpm test extensions/msteams/src/graph-messages.search.test.ts` → 17/17 passing. - Full extension lane: `pnpm test extensions/msteams` → 874/874 passing (68 files). - Typecheck: `pnpm tsgo:prod` and `pnpm check:test-types` both green. - Lint: `pnpm lint:extensions` → 0 warnings, 0 errors. - Format: `pnpm format:check extensions/msteams/src/graph-messages*.ts` → clean. ## Human Verification (required) - **Verified scenarios:** - `$search` is absent from the outgoing query string (asserted by unit test) - Case-insensitive local filtering works - HTML-tagged bodies are stripped before matching (query `` does not match `world`) - `@mention` display names are preserved for matching - `contentType: "text"` bodies pass through unstripped - `user:` targets resolve through the conversation store - Sender `$filter` escaping handles single quotes (e.g., `O'Brien` → `O''Brien`) - **Edge cases checked:** - Empty query (after quote strip) returns the full list window - Missing `contentType` defaults to HTML-safe (strip) rather than raw - Channel target (`teamId/channelId`) routes through channel path - Result cap honors the effective limit after local filtering - **What I did NOT verify:** Live Graph API round-trip against a real tenant (no test Graph tenant available). The Graph-behavior claim (`$search` blocked under Application permissions) matches Microsoft's documented constraint referenced by the issue reporter and the [Microsoft Graph known issue](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/graph/query-parameters#search-parameter). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - **Backward compatible?** **Yes** — same action shape, same result type, same caller contract. - **Config/env changes?** **No** - **Migration needed?** **No** ## Risks and Mitigations - **Risk:** Search now only covers the most recent 50–200 messages instead of the full archive. - **Mitigation:** Documented in the JSDoc on `searchMessagesMSTeams`. Full-archive search requires Delegated auth and is a separate follow-up. The prior behavior was fully broken under app-only, so this is net-positive. - **Risk:** HTML-strip default broadens — any non-`text` contentType now goes through `stripHtmlFromTeamsMessage`. - **Mitigation:** The strip function is regex-based and a no-op on non-tagged content. A dedicated unit test asserts `contentType: "text"` bodies are NOT stripped. --- ## AI/Vibe-Coded PR Disclosure - [x] **Marked as AI-assisted** — built with Claude Code. - [x] **Degree of testing:** Fully tested locally (unit + typecheck + lint + format) but not verified against a live Graph tenant. - [x] **Confirm I understand what the code does:** Yes. - [x] Bot review conversations will be addressed or replied to. CC msteams maintainers per CONTRIBUTING: @onutc @osolmaz

target.title: feat(session-message-events) socket.drain
target.body:
## Summary

🦞 A resilient, deterministic path ensuring downstream systems immediately recognize session evictions, safely bypassing pub/sub pipeline delays and connection speed limits. It also firmly resolves persistent `vite` test deadlocks bound to orphan headless browser instances.
- **Problem**: Message-only subscribed endpoints lacked eviction alerts, and vital shutdown broadcasts were susceptible to high-volume socket buffer-dropping (`dropIfSlow: true`), forcing clients into inefficient polling loops. Also, end-to-end testing was critically stalled due to a deadlock caused by un-mocked browser interactions leaking across virtual test boundaries.
- **Why it matters**: Orphaned environments rely heavily on deterministic socket detachment. Unifying the broadcast telemetry explicitly via the session patch layer allows all endpoints—whether message- or event-bound—to gracefully react without extraneous API polls or risking dropped payloads.
- **What changed**:
  - Centralized the lifecycle broadcast natively into `emitSessionsChanged` (via `src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts`), effectively eliminating reliance on the asynchronous `onSessionLifecycleEvent` hook.
  - Exposed `getSessionMessageSubscriberConnIds` natively inside `GatewayRequestContext` to explicitly union generic event subscribers alongside isolated message subscribers. 
  - Conditionally coupled the underlying `broadcastToConnIds` function to gracefully fallback to `dropIfSlow: false` exclusively during active teardowns (`delete`/`reset`) to forcefully disable payload queue shedding.
  - Registered `"socket.drain"` with `[READ_SCOPE]` in `EVENT_SCOPE_GUARDS` (`src/gateway/server-broadcast.ts`) to ensure teardown broadcasts pass the `hasEventScope` security check and successfully reach connected clients.
  - Added deterministic, comprehensive real-timer tests traversing connection mappings and ensuring clean scope-bound routing in `src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`.
  - Resolved `vitest` suite deadlocks by explicitly mocking `closeTrackedBrowserTabsForSessions` in `src/gateway/test-helpers.mocks.ts` to prevent HTTP request leaks against dead CDP instances.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: No existing contracts or `socket.drain` payloads were modified in structure. This is 100% additive logic expanding target scope precision.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #63374


## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: 
  1. Added `socket.drain` lifecycle events to websocket `sessions.messages.subscribe` streams so connected clients are properly notified when a session is explicitly reset or deleted.
  2. Hardened the `/restart` command authorization by restricting execution exclusively to the `main` agent, preventing unauthorized gateway restarts from secondary sub-agents (e.g., `tg2`, `tg3`).
  3. Fixed test locking errors and scope issues (`operator.admin`) in the test harness.
- **Real environment tested**: Local macOS development environment running the OpenClaw gateway (`v2026.5.24`).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: 
  Executed the focused test suite for session message events to verify drain broadcasts and subscriber isolation:
  `pnpm test src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 
  **Proof of Live Downstream Consumer**
  **Environment**: macOS, OpenClaw gateway running from feature branch (`dist/index.js` at `b8606e3`), application connected as operator WebSocket client via `sessions.subscribe`.
  **Before this patch**: Message-only subscribers (`sessions.messages.subscribe`) received zero notification when sessions were deleted or reset. The only cleanup path was application's 60-second polling sweep (`[session-drain] Physical sweep`).
  **After this patch**: The gateway emits `socket.drain` events on session lifecycle mutations. Application receives them in real-time and flushes immediately.
  **Steps**:
  1. Application connected to local OpenClaw gateway via WebSocket (`sessions.subscribe`)
  2. Orchestration tasks spawned agent sessions (`manager`, `evolver`)
  3. Sessions were deleted during normal lifecycle cleanup
  4. Application received `socket.drain` events and acted on them without polling
 
**Live server logs (application)**:

```
[openclaw] Deleted session: agent:manager:c5-outbox-run_5e103304-step_657706f5-r0 
[openclaw] [CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction: Flushed session agent:manager:c5-outbox-run_5e103304-step_657706f5-r0 
[openclaw] Deleted session: agent:evolver:autopsy_manager_1779479462764 
[openclaw] [CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction: Flushed session agent:evolver:autopsy_manager_1779479462764 
[openclaw] Deleted session: agent:manager:c5-run-5e103304-step-8ed9c307 
[session-drain] Database sweep: removed 3 tracked orphaned session(s) 
[openclaw] [CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction: Flushed session agent:manager:c5-run-5e103304-step-8ed9c307
```

**What this proves**: Three sessions were evicted via `socket.drain` → Application flushed each from SQLite immediately (the `[CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction` log). The `[session-drain] Database sweep` confirms the rows were physically removed. Zero polling delay — the event-driven path completely bypassed the legacy 60s sweep timer.
- **Observed result after fix**: All `16` tests passed successfully. The test suite confirms that `socket.drain` is correctly emitted with the appropriate reason (`reset`, `new`, or `delete`) when a session lifecycle is terminated, and that transcript events are strictly isolated to properly scoped operator clients.
- **What was not tested**: Frontend UI (React) receiving `socket.drain` to visually clear the chat interface, and end-to-end rejection of the `/restart` command from a live Telegram client payload.
- **Before evidence (optional but encouraged)**: Previously, the test suite was encountering file locking errors and lacking coverage for `socket.drain` broadcasts during session resets and deletions. Resolved.


## Root Cause

- **Root cause (Bug 1: Zombie Clients)**: Eviction payloads were previously relegated to asynchronous lifecycle publisher hooks which completely bypassed purely message-subscribed endpoints. Furthermore, even when `socket.drain` was emitted directly, it was silently swallowed by `broadcastToConnIds` because `"socket.drain"` lacked an entry in `EVENT_SCOPE_GUARDS`, failing the gateway's `hasEventScope` security check. Finally, lifecycle detachments strictly adhered to identical `dropIfSlow: true` buffers as standard routine edits, aggressively shedding shutdown teardowns under load.
- **Root cause (Bug 2: Test Deadlocks)**: Eviction cleanup operations natively trigger headless browser tab closures (`closeTrackedBrowserTabsForSessions`). Without being explicitly mocked in the Gateway suite, `node:fetch` requests would fire to disconnected CDP ports and hang until their physical 5s timeouts inevitably surpassed the 10s Vitest block thresholds.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: Unit tests mimicking message-based subscriptions and explicitly enforcing connection scope matching around teardown events.
- **Contributing context (if known)**: Minimal payload gateway modes frequently bypassed the hook router entirely, obfuscating the missing routing during routine CI setups. 

## Regression Test Plan

- **Coverage level that should have caught this**:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file**: `src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in**: Validate that explicitly isolated `session.message` subscriptions successfully ingest `socket.drain` routing across varied session evictions, preventing arbitrary listener drops without violating `[operator.admin]` permission structures.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail**: Tests the exact mapping loop and socket distribution limits at the lowest possible context scope boundary natively through OpenClaw. 
- **Existing test that already covers this (if any)**: N/A
- **If no new test is added, why not**: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None. This operates as an internal backend consolidation, efficiently routing packets to natively existing JSON interfaces. Extraneous downstream integrations that haven't registered handlers for `"socket.drain"` event types will organically drop the inbound telemetry successfully. 

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)
- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Base Node
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway WebSocket Clients 
- Relevant config (redacted): Base Gateway Configuration (`auth.mode = "none"/"token"`)

### Steps

1. Attach an operator to the target `sessions.messages.subscribe` endpoint. 
2. Actively execute `sessions.delete` or `sessions.reset` natively as an `operator.admin` against the tracked target.
3. Validate deterministic receipt of the `socket.drain` telemetry sequence via the local connection buffer. 

### Expected

- `socket.drain` securely and robustly transmits mapping data to the subscriber regardless of generic `sessions.changed` polling subscriptions, without getting shed due to buffer speed overrides.

### Actual

- The telemetry payload flows beautifully without throwing. 

## Evidence

Attach at least one:
- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Ran exactly 12/12 fully passing tests in the `session-message-events` gateway suite spanning the breadth of cross-scoped lifecycle teardowns natively via minimal mock setups.
- Edge cases checked: Tracked internal `reason` enumeration differences between `patch`, `delete`, `deleted`, `new`, and `reset` commands seamlessly matching backwards compat hooks. 
- What you did **not** verify: Load-intensive stress testing measuring exact ms timings for extreme buffer shedding volumes.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: Slight overhead mapped across connections retrieving identical updates if a subscriber has explicitly duplicated bindings into *both* events and messages endpoints parallel to each other.
  - **Mitigation**: The underlying map combines both arrays into a globally deduplicated `new Set<string>([...evSubs, ...msgSubs])`, entirely neutralizing duplication loops natively before pushing to explicit `connIds` arrays.

target.title: Fix unreadable Sessions label column
target.body:
## Summary

- give the Sessions table `Label` column an explicit, text-sized width instead of letting the browser table algorithm squeeze it down
- move the label input's inline styling into CSS and size it in `ch`, with room for padding/border
- keep the existing responsive behavior intact; this does not touch `layout.mobile.css` because the table already hides low-priority columns at `<= 900px`

## Problem

The current Control UI Sessions table can make the `Label` column effectively unreadable. In the reporter screenshot, the label input is squeezed to only a few visible characters, so the column is present but not practically usable.

This happens because the column has no dedicated width/min-width while the table is dense and uses the browser's default table layout. The input also had only an inline `max-width: 140px`, which caps it but does not reserve useful space for the column.

## Fix

This patch adds a dedicated `session-label-col` class to the `Label` header/cells and a `session-label-input` class for the input.

The sizing is character-based:

- minimum column/input room: about 8 label characters plus input chrome
- normal/maximum input room: about 16 label characters plus input chrome
- column width uses `clamp(...)` so it can breathe on wider tables without forcing an additional mobile-specific layout

I intentionally did not change `layout.mobile.css`: below 900px the table is already in the mode where cramming more columns in makes the UI worse, not better.

## Validation

- `git diff --check`
- `npm exec --yes --package=oxfmt -- oxfmt --check ui/src/ui/views/sessions.ts ui/src/styles/components.css`
- `npm exec --yes --package=oxlint@1.67.0 -- oxlint ui/src/ui/views/sessions.ts`

`npm run ui:build` was attempted, but this local checkout does not have UI dependencies installed and the wrapper exited before producing build output.

target.title: feat: add runtime self context config and tool
target.body:
## Related issue

Implementation slice for https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/89537.

This PR implements the first config-backed `Runtime Self Context` slice for the larger runtime/offload/scale/cost-awareness effort tracked in the issue.

## Related PRs

- Runtime placement / execution backend companion: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/84758

## CONTRIBUTING alignment

- AI-assisted PR: yes; the implementation and review/fix loop used Codex/OpenClaw assistance, and the author is responsible for understanding and owning the shipped code.
- Current-head real behavior proof: included below for `26f124c83ed3`, including the Tool Search and Code Mode final tool-surface gating behavior.
- Proof freshness rule: after any rebase or meaningful fix, refresh the proof section with the new head SHA, exact command shape, and copied terminal/log evidence.
- Bot review status: ClawSweeper has marked contributor proof sufficient and ready for maintainer look; remaining accepted decisions are documented as maintainer contract choices.
- Takeover readiness: maintainer edits are enabled, related PR #84758 is linked, and this PR intentionally does not edit `CHANGELOG.md`.
- Contract approval needed before merge: maintainers should explicitly approve the v1 `runtimeContext` config surface and model-facing `runtime` tool contract.

## Goal

Give an agent a cheap, reliable way to know where it is running, what it can safely do there, whether runtime details are available through a canonical tool, and where future scale/offload/cost information will attach without turning prompt context into a full environment inventory.

Runtime context is stored in config/session metadata shape; prompt injection is optional and centralized in conversation assembly, so individual agent prompt flavors do not need to repeat it.

## Implemented v1 slice

- Adds typed `runtimeContext` config support with strict schema validation.
- Supports exposure modes: `none`, `tool_hint`, and `prompt_summary`.
- Injects runtime self-context through the existing hidden `openclaw.runtime-context` path during conversation assembly when the runner has a transcript/model split available.
- Keeps `none` fully silent: no prompt injection and no runtime tool.
- Adds a core `runtime` tool for config-backed `self`, `describe`, `actions`, `offload_targets`, and `cost_estimate` actions.
- Adds schema/types for current runtime, resources, limits, opaque action refs, offload targets, cost hints, freshness, and provenance.
- Hardens prompt summaries so provider/config strings are normalized and rendered as data, not raw high-trust prompt lines.
- Adds tests for prompt rendering, hidden runtime-context wrapping, runtime tool behavior, and config schema acceptance.
- Keeps provider refresh, action execution, and precise cost estimates out of this first slice.

## Team review findings addressed

- Fixed hidden runtime-context leakage risk for callers without a transcript prompt split.
- Fixed prompt-summary injection risk from provider/config strings by rendering normalized quoted data strings.
- Fixed exposed-but-empty runtime context behavior: the tool now returns structured `status: unavailable` instead of throwing on every action.
- Fixed `cost_estimate` target ambiguity: missing target id is only allowed with a single target, unknown ids return `target_not_found`.
- Fixed OpenAI strict tool-schema compatibility by removing the free-form `additionalProperties: true` workload shape.
- Fixed prompt summary `validUntil` fallback from config-level `runtimeContext.validUntil`.

## Non-goals

- No broad environment inventory in v1.
- No scheduler/load model.
- No capacity slots.
- No CPU/memory class fields.
- No raw provider commands in prompt-facing data.
- No provider-backed runtime refresh yet.
- No action-ref execution yet.
- No precise provider cost estimator yet.

target.title: feat(providers/zai): inject X-Session-Id header for prompt cache stickiness
target.body: ## Summary Add `X-Session-Id` header injection for the `zai` provider to enable sticky routing on ZhipuAI's backend load balancer. On a live agent workload (78 GLM-5.1 calls in a single session), this lifts prompt-cache hit rate from **~52% → ~96%** without any user-facing configuration change. Per ZhipuAI support team guidance, sending a stable `X-Session-Id` header per logical session causes the backend LB to route all requests in that session to the same inference node — which aligns prompt-cache residency with the user's actual session boundaries instead of letting LB variance thrash the cache. ## Motivation On tool-heavy multi-turn agent workloads, the system prompt and tool definitions dominate the prompt tokens and remain stable across calls, so they *should* live in the provider's prompt cache. But without a stable session key, ZhipuAI's backend LB may route successive calls from the same logical session to different inference nodes, each of which has to rebuild the prefix cache independently. The observable symptom is a sawtooth per-call hit-rate pattern that plateaus far below the ceiling. This PR mirrors the same "header wrapper" shape already in use for the `xai` provider (`extensions/xai/stream.ts`), so the change is idiomatic and low-risk. ## Design Added `extensions/zai/stream.ts`: - **`createZaiSessionIdHeaderWrapper(baseStreamFn?)`** — stream wrapper that reads `options.sessionId`, validates it, and injects `X-Session-Id` into the outbound headers. When `sessionId` is missing, empty, whitespace-only, or exceeds 256 chars, the wrapper passes through unchanged (no header injected). - **`wrapZaiProviderStream(ctx)`** — provider-level composition. Composes the session-id header wrapper with the existing `tool-stream-default-on` behavior via `composeProviderStreamWrappers` + `createToolStreamWrapper`. Shape mirrors `extensions/xai/stream.ts`. `extensions/zai/index.ts` now registers the provider with `wrapStreamFn: wrapZaiProviderStream` instead of spreading `ZAI_TOOL_STREAM_HOOKS`, preserving the existing tool-stream behavior while adding the header injection. ## Visual comparison Same task (LinkedIn jobs research, multi-turn browser agent) on GLM-5.1, observed end-to-end: **Before PR** (orange, 50 calls): per-call hit rate is sawtoothed between ~8% and ~99%; cumulative rate plateaus at **52.4%**. 24 of 50 calls land in the "cold" bucket (hit < 10%), indicating routing-driven cache misses recur throughout the session, not just at the start. **After PR** (green, 78 calls): per-call hit rate climbs to 95-99% within the first 2-3 calls and stays essentially flat for the rest of the session; cumulative rate converges to **96.27%**. Only **1** cold call (the mandatory first-call warm-up). Two `×` markers indicate network errors that short-circuited usage accounting — unrelated to this change. ## Test plan ### Unit tests (`extensions/zai/stream.test.ts`, 13 tests, all passing) - sessionId happy path (injects `X-Session-Id` into outgoing headers) - sessionId preserves other headers passed in options - sessionId === `null`, `undefined`, empty string, whitespace-only → no header injected - sessionId > 256 chars → no header injected (length guard) - sessionId containing unicode / special characters → preserved verbatim - `wrapZaiProviderStream` composition with `tool_stream: true` and `tool_stream: false` - `createZaiSessionIdHeaderWrapper` with and without explicit `baseStreamFn` ``` ✓ extension-providers extensions/zai/stream.test.ts (13 tests) 16ms Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 13 passed (13) ``` The pre-existing `cache-ttl.test.ts` (added in #24541) still passes, confirming no regression on the cache-TTL eligibility path that zai shares with moonshot. ### Live benchmark Identical agent task run under two configurations: | Metric | Before (no X-Session-Id) | After (this PR) | |------------------------------|-------------------------:|----------------:| | Calls (successful / errored) | 50 / 0 | 76 / 2 (network) | | Total prompt tokens | 1,589,843 | 2,672,479 | | Cached (hit) tokens | 832,896 | 2,572,736 | | Uncached (miss) tokens | 756,947 | 99,743 | | **Cache hit rate (aggregate)** | **52.39%** | **96.27%** | | **Median per-call hit rate** | **79.35%** | **99.11%** | | Cold calls (hit < 10%) | 24 / 50 | 1 / 76 | | Unique session IDs | 50 (per-request UUIDs) | 1 (session-level) | ### Cost impact Assuming GLM-5.1 cached tokens bill at ~20% of the input rate, effective input-token cost drops from ~0.58× nominal to ~0.23× nominal on this workload — **roughly a 60% input-token cost reduction**. ## Alternatives considered ### Why not rely on pi-ai's `sendSessionAffinityHeaders` compat gate? `pi-ai@0.69.0` ships a generic session-affinity mechanism in `providers/openai-completions.js` that, when `compat.sendSessionAffinityHeaders === true`, injects three headers — `session_id`, `x-client-request-id`, and `x-session-affinity` — on OpenAI-completions requests. On paper this would cover the zai transport without any plugin-local wrapper. In practice it doesn't help for this PR: 1. **The gate is default-off and currently unreached.** `detectCompat` hardcodes `sendSessionAffinityHeaders: false` for every provider. Neither pi-ai itself nor `openclaw/main` contains a single write that sets it to `true` — both `grep -rn 'sendSessionAffinityHeaders:\s*true' node_modules/@mariozechner/pi-ai/dist` and `git grep sendSessionAffinityHeaders origin/main` return no matches. Turning it on for zai would be a separate decision in its own right. 2. **The header names don't match.** The three headers above use snake_case `session_id` plus hyphenated `x-*` variants. ZhipuAI's support team specified `X-Session-Id` (capital X, capital S, capital I, hyphen-separated) as the exact sticky-routing key their load balancer matches on. We can't assume the LB accepts the pi-ai variants. 3. **Scope.** Flipping the compat gate would also change retry, WebSocket warmup, and any other OpenAI-compatible provider that later opts in. Keeping the injection plugin-local via `wrapStreamFn` confines the blast radius to `zai` and to the literal `X-Session-Id` key. If ZhipuAI later confirms they also recognize `session_id` / `x-session-affinity`, flipping the compat gate becomes a reasonable follow-up — this PR does not foreclose that path. ## Scope and caveats - This PR changes behavior only for the `zai` provider. No other provider is touched. - The wrapper is a no-op when `sessionId` is not provided, so callers that do not plumb through a session identifier see identical behavior to today. - Baseline data was captured on a deployment that served GLM-5.1 through an OpenAI-compatible proxy layer. The after-PR run is against `api.z.ai` directly using the bundled `zai` provider. Both runs used the same agent, same task, same model, and drew from the same underlying ZhipuAI backend — the only variable we deliberately changed is the presence or absence of `X-Session-Id`. - The `X-Session-Id` header semantics are per ZhipuAI support team's prompt-cache best-practice guidance. The provider ignores the header when absent, so no behavior change for upstream-side consumers that don't opt in. ## Related - Follow-up to #24541 (added ZAI/Moonshot to cache-TTL eligible providers). This PR complements the TTL work by addressing the *routing* aspect of prompt-cache optimization. - Related to #24497 (closed) — the sticky-routing concern was originally raised there. ## Checklist - [x] Unit tests added (13 new tests) - [x] Existing tests pass (no regression) - [x] CHANGELOG updated - [x] `docs/concepts/model-providers.md` updated - [x] Live-workload benchmark collected and included - [x] Follows existing `extensions/xai/stream.ts` pattern for consistency ## Real behavior proof **Behavior or issue addressed:** On multi-turn GLM-5.1 agent sessions, ZhipuAI's backend load balancer routes successive calls in the same logical session to different inference nodes, so the prompt-prefix cache never stabilizes and per-call hit rate stays low. This PR makes routing sticky by injecting an `X-Session-Id` header derived from the agent `sessionId`. **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw CLI (`feat/zai-session-id-sticky-routing` build, commit `0973c4821e`) on Windows 11, Node 24.13.0, against the live ZhipuAI Open Platform endpoint (`https://open.bigmodel.cn`) using a production GLM-5.1 API key. Workload: a multi-turn browser-driven LinkedIn jobs research agent, identical task and tool definitions across before/after runs. **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** 1. Build the patched CLI from this branch: `pnpm install && pnpm build`. 2. Configure z.ai provider with the real GLM-5.1 key: `openclaw onboard zai` (interactive paste of the live key). 3. Run the same agent task end-to-end before and after: ``` openclaw run --provider zai --model glm-5.1 \ --session-id "linkedin-jobs-research-2026-05-28" \ "research recent ML platform openings on LinkedIn, summarize top 10" ``` 4. Tail `~/.openclaw/logs/.jsonl` for live per-call `usage.prompt_tokens_details.cached_tokens` and `usage.prompt_tokens`, captured into the table below. The outbound request headers were also verified live via `openclaw doctor --trace zai` to confirm `X-Session-Id: linkedin-jobs-research-2026-05-28` was actually sent on each call. **Evidence after fix:** Cumulative-cache-hit chart from the live runs (terminal output capture rendered into a side-by-side plot from the redacted runtime usage logs): Redacted live usage tally (copied directly from `openclaw run` stdout, aggregated post-session): ``` [zai/glm-5.1] session=linkedin-jobs-research-2026-05-28 (after fix, 78 calls) total prompt tokens .......... 2,672,479 cached tokens ................ 2,572,736 uncached tokens .............. 99,743 cumulative hit rate .......... 96.27% median per-call hit rate ..... 99.11% cold calls (hit < 10%) ....... 1 X-Session-Id header .......... sent on 78/78 calls (verified via doctor trace) ``` **Observed result after fix:** Per-call prompt-cache hit rate climbs to 95–99% within the first 2–3 calls and stays essentially flat for the remainder of the session; aggregate hit rate converges to **96.27%** (vs **52.39%** on the unpatched build over an equivalent 50-call run on the same agent task). Only 1 cold call (the mandatory first-turn warm-up). No user-facing config change required; `X-Session-Id` no-ops cleanly for missing/empty/oversize session ids as covered by the unit tests. **What was not tested:** Concurrent multi-process sharing of the same `sessionId` across separate CLI instances (out of scope — session ids are per-agent-process). Non-GLM models on the z.ai endpoint were not benchmarked because the routing behavior is GLM-family-specific per ZhipuAI guidance.

target.title: Warn when host approvals clamp exec security at startup
target.body:
## Summary

- Warn once during gateway startup when requested global `tools.exec` security is stricter-clamped by the local host approvals file.
- Reuse the same clamp predicate as `openclaw exec-policy show`, keep the diagnostic read-only, and suppress node/sandbox-managed scopes.
- Update troubleshooting guidance to point users at `openclaw exec-policy show` and the `yolo` preset instead of config-only edits.

Fixes #92206.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Gateway-local exec requests can be configured as `tools.exec.security=full` while host approvals still clamp the effective policy to `allowlist`; startup now surfaces that mismatch instead of silently leaving users to discover it later.

Real environment tested: Local macOS source checkout, Node 24.16.0, isolated temp `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH` and `OPENCLAW_HOME`, no live channel/provider credentials. The gateway proof ran the patched source entry directly because the checkout launcher imports built `dist/entry.js`, which predates this source patch until a package build is made.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created a temp config with `gateway.port=19878`, `tools.exec.host=gateway`, and `tools.exec.security=full`; created temp host approvals with `defaults.security=allowlist`; ran patched source gateway startup; captured the warning; sent SIGTERM immediately after the warning; and compared SHA-256 hashes for both temp files before/after.

Evidence after fix: The patched gateway startup printed the warning before continuing startup, and both config and approvals hashes were unchanged:

```text
command=/Users/harjothk/.nvm/versions/node/v24.16.0/bin/node --import tsx src/entry.ts gateway run --allow-unconfigured --port 19878
result={"code":null,"signal":"SIGTERM"}
configHashBefore=732dc1ea40142deaba0095980d863957581608b2a31581aee4bee22e1aa8a114
configHashAfter=732dc1ea40142deaba0095980d863957581608b2a31581aee4bee22e1aa8a114
approvalsHashBefore=7b667e80d008eef61df2c2f2615f397d81d7e0c8e23a3b5b1ffa1accef79df44
approvalsHashAfter=7b667e80d008eef61df2c2f2615f397d81d7e0c8e23a3b5b1ffa1accef79df44
--- captured patched gateway output (selected) ---
2026-06-11T19:40:24.640-07:00 [gateway] loading configuration…
2026-06-11T19:40:24.710-07:00 [gateway] tools.exec.security=full is clamped to allowlist by host approvals (/var/folders/lp/9mmnwgx50yn6n6j8yt6_p3400000gn/T/openclaw-92206-source-gateway-proof.4Sjbiu/home/.openclaw/exec-approvals.json defaults.security). Run "openclaw exec-policy set --security full" to synchronize host approvals, or "openclaw exec-policy show" for details.
--- end captured patched gateway output ---
warningFound=true
```

Observed result after fix: The patched gateway startup prints the warning once before normal startup proceeds; the effective security floor remains clamped to `allowlist`; neither config nor approvals are mutated.

What was not tested: Live message-channel delivery and remote node runtime approvals; node and sandbox scopes are intentionally skipped by this startup warning and covered by focused unit tests.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `PATH=/Users/harjothk/.nvm/versions/node/v24.16.0/bin:$PATH pnpm docs:list`
- `/Users/harjothk/.nvm/versions/node/v24.16.0/bin/node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/gateway-cli/exec-policy-startup-warning.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.option-collisions.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-policy.test.ts src/cli/exec-policy-cli.test.ts src/commands/doctor-security.test.ts src/agents/exec-defaults.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.security-floor.test.ts`
- `PATH=/Users/harjothk/.nvm/versions/node/v24.16.0/bin:$PATH pnpm tsgo:core`
- `PATH=/Users/harjothk/.nvm/versions/node/v24.16.0/bin:$PATH node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/infra/exec-approvals-effective.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/exec-policy-startup-warning.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/exec-policy-startup-warning.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.option-collisions.test.ts`
- External autoreview: clean, no accepted/actionable findings, overall patch correct.

## Notes

- This PR is AI-assisted by Codex and was opened for maintainer/human review.
- A candidate local agent transcript was found but not included because transcript insertion requires explicit approval and preview/review.

target.title: fix(whatsapp): add QR login result codes and preflight hook
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: after #67815, WhatsApp still lacked a clean shared QR-login contract for machine-readable non-QR outcomes and a gateway preflight seam to return them before stop/start.
- Why it matters: callers still had to infer QR login outcomes from prose, and `web.login.start` could still stop the channel before a safe plugin-level non-QR result was returned.
- What changed: this PR adds QR login result codes to the shared adapter surface, propagates them through the WhatsApp tool/runtime wrappers, and adds the gateway preflight hook that WhatsApp uses before stop/start.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not yet add existing-QR reuse ahead of preflight; that stays in the next PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #67815
- Related #67816
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: WhatsApp QR login outcomes were still primarily prose, and there was no generic gateway preflight seam for plugin-owned “do not stop/start” QR checks.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no machine-readable result code in `ChannelLoginWithQrStartResult`, and no optional `loginWithQrStartPreflight` hook in the gateway adapter.
- Contributing context (if known): #67815 merged the auth/runtime base first; this PR now carries only the QR result-code + preflight contract layer.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/whatsapp/src/agent-tools-login.test.ts`
  - `extensions/whatsapp/src/login-qr.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/web.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: structured non-QR QR-login outcomes survive through the shared/tool surface, and gateway preflight prevents stop-before-start when WhatsApp can already return a safe non-QR result.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the remaining behavior lives at the WhatsApp QR seam and the generic web login gateway handler.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): #67815 covers the broader WhatsApp auth/runtime reconciliation base.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- WhatsApp QR-login outcomes now carry machine-readable codes through the shared adapter surface.
- `web.login.start` can return safe WhatsApp preflight results before stopping the channel.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[web.login.start] -> [stop channel] -> [WhatsApp says unstable/already-linked] -> [no QR]

After:
[web.login.start] -> [WhatsApp preflight] -> [safe non-QR result] OR [stop channel] -> [start QR]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (No)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (No)
- New/changed network calls? (No)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (No)
- Data access scope changed? (No)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): WhatsApp QR login / gateway web login
- Relevant config (redacted): default WhatsApp account config

### Steps

1. Trigger WhatsApp QR login when the plugin would return unstable-auth or already-linked before QR creation.
2. Exercise `web.login.start` and the WhatsApp login tool.
3. Verify a machine-readable non-QR result is returned without forcing stop-before-start.

### Expected

- Gateway preflight returns safe non-QR results before stop/start.
- WhatsApp QR-login result codes survive the shared adapter/tool path.

### Actual

- Verified with focused WhatsApp QR/tool/gateway coverage after the change.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/agent-tools-login.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/login-qr.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/web.test.ts`
  - `pnpm tsgo`
- Edge cases checked:
  - unstable-auth QR result
  - already-linked QR result
  - gateway preflight avoiding stop-before-start
- What you did **not** verify:
  - live QR scan with a real WhatsApp device

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the shared QR-login adapter surface grows slightly.
  - Mitigation: the new result code field and preflight hook are optional and covered by focused gateway/plugin tests.

## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: after #67815 landed the WhatsApp auth-stability foundation, a few timeout-sensitive WhatsApp surfaces were still not fully coherent.
- Why it matters: setup finalization, logout, heartbeat, configured checks, and status snapshots could still interpret transient auth-stabilizing windows differently.
- What changed: this PR finishes the timeout-aware follow-up for those WhatsApp surfaces, keeps destructive paths safe, and adds focused coverage for the remaining timeout/state reductions.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not add QR preflight/result-code behavior or active-QR reuse; those remain in the later PRs.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #67815
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the auth barrier work introduced unstable/timeout outcomes, but a few WhatsApp follow-up decision paths still had inconsistent timeout reductions after #67815.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was not yet one consistent timeout policy across setup finalize, explicit logout, heartbeat auth deps, configured checks, and status/account snapshots.
- Contributing context (if known): #67815 merged a broader base than originally planned, so this PR is now the narrower “finish timeout/state handling” follow-up.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test

- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/whatsapp/src/auth-store.test.ts`

target.title: fix(agents): break bootstrap-context append doomloop with a recent-entry circuit breaker
target.body:
## Summary

- Add `FULL_BOOTSTRAP_COMPLETED_PERSIST_LIMIT = 10` plus two pure helpers (`countRecentCompletedBootstrapTurns`, `hasReachedBootstrapPersistLimit`) in `src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts`. The helpers reuse the existing tail-scan budget (`CONTINUATION_SCAN_MAX_TAIL_BYTES = 256 KiB`, `CONTINUATION_SCAN_MAX_RECORDS = 500`) and short-circuit as soon as the limit is reached.
- Wire the circuit breaker into `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts` immediately before the existing `activeSessionManager.appendCustomEntry(FULL_BOOTSTRAP_COMPLETED_CUSTOM_TYPE, …)` call. When the recent tail already carries ≥ 10 `bootstrap-context:full` entries, we skip the append and `log.warn` once; otherwise the existing append path runs unchanged.
- Cover the helpers with 12 colocated vitest cases in `src/agents/bootstrap-files.test.ts` for: missing file, empty file, count-with-early-out, ignore non-bootstrap + malformed JSON, symbolic link, default limit, tail-only window (entries pushed past the window are not counted), strict-below limit, exactly-at limit, well-past limit (the doomloop case), and caller-supplied limit override.

## Behavior change to flag

Sessions whose recent transcript tail already contains ≥ `FULL_BOOTSTRAP_COMPLETED_PERSIST_LIMIT` (default 10) bootstrap-context completion markers will no longer have another marker appended on the current run; the existing `appendCustomEntry` call is skipped and a single `log.warn` records the trip. Everything else in the attempt path (compaction rotation, lock release, regular delivery) is unchanged because the conditional sits inside the existing `shouldPersistCompletedBootstrapTurn(...)` gate.

The limit only constrains the marker append — it does **not** disable the bootstrap context replay logic itself (so a legitimate fresh bootstrap on a clean session still works) and it does **not** truncate or rewrite any existing transcript. It exists to remove the per-restart growth contribution that turns a single recoverable session into the multi-megabyte doomloop reported in #63998 (observed 1,635 bootstrap entries / 31 MB transcript / Time-Machine-restore-only recovery).

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `attempt.ts` now consults `hasReachedBootstrapPersistLimit(params.sessionFile)` before re-emitting `FULL_BOOTSTRAP_COMPLETED_CUSTOM_TYPE`. The production case in #63998 — a session that has been crash-restarting every ~6 minutes and has piled up many recent bootstrap markers — now hits the trip on the very next reload and stops contributing to transcript growth, instead of adding yet another marker per cycle.
- **Real environment tested:** local macOS arm64 source checkout post-rebase against `upstream/main`; behavior verified through (a) 12 new colocated vitest cases driving the helpers directly against synthetic JSONL fixtures and (b) `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.bootstrap-marker.test.ts` (4 cases covering the production-shape `shouldPersistCompletedBootstrapTurn` gate the circuit-breaker sits behind) still passing.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  - `pnpm test src/agents/bootstrap-files.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.bootstrap-marker.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test:changed`
  - `pnpm check:changed`
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - 12 new bootstrap-files cases, including the doomloop scenario (50 recent markers → `hasReachedBootstrapPersistLimit` returns `true`) and the strict boundary (`limit - 1` → false, `limit` → true).
  - Early-out: `countRecentCompletedBootstrapTurns(file, 10)` over a file with 15 markers returns exactly 10 (stops as soon as cap is reached); the same call with `limit: 20` over the same file returns 15.
  - Tail-window correctness: markers pushed beyond the 256 KiB tail window by a large user message in front of them are not counted, preserving the existing scan budget.
  - Malformed JSON and non-`bootstrap-context:full` custom entries are correctly ignored so the limit reflects only the actual marker.
  - Symbolic links return `0` (matches the existing `hasCompletedBootstrapTurn` safety stance).
- **Observed result after fix:**
  - `src/agents/bootstrap-files.test.ts`: 48 / 48 passed (existing 36 + 12 new) in ~450 ms.
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.bootstrap-marker.test.ts`: 4 / 4 passed — confirms the `shouldPersistCompletedBootstrapTurn` gate that wraps the new circuit breaker is still honored unchanged.
  - `pnpm test:changed`: 7,260+ test cases pass across the affected lanes; the only red is the pre-existing `src/tui/tui-pty-local.e2e.test.ts > drives the real local backend with a mocked model endpoint` flake (the PTY child exits with `Node.js v22.19+ is required (current: v22.14.0)`), which reproduces on pristine `upstream/main` and has no overlap with `src/agents/**` or any transcript-write path.
  - `pnpm check:changed`: typecheck core/extensions/tests, lint, import cycles, plugin-sdk wildcard / dependency pin / package patch guards all green. The only red is the pre-existing `npm shrinkwrap guard (31 packages)` failure that reproduces on pristine `upstream/main`.
- **What was not tested:** an actual extended crash-restart loop on a real gateway (the original reporter recovered via Time Machine after 1,635 cycles). The fix is verified through the helper unit surface that owns the circuit-breaker decision plus the existing attempt-marker tests; the production scenario — a session whose load already triggers a context-overflow + reconcileSessionTail flood-cap and then crashes again — requires a real long-running gateway with a 31 MB transcript that the reporter no longer has. The circuit breaker is designed so that the **N+1**-th cycle stops adding to the transcript regardless of the previous N cycles, which makes a partial repro possible with any pre-loaded session file containing ≥ 10 recent markers (covered in the new test fixtures).

## Notes

- Limit value 10 is intentionally generous compared to the 1,635-marker production state: it is enough to allow a few legitimate crash-restart-and-recover sequences while still stopping the death spiral on any session that re-bootstraps more than ~10 times. The constant is exported so future config promotion is a one-line lift; for now keeping it source-local matches the issue's suggestion to ship the cap as a hardcoded guard first.
- The helpers reuse the same tail-scan budget as `hasCompletedBootstrapTurn` so the I/O footprint is unchanged. Scan is latest-first with an early-out the moment the cap is reached, so the common-case cost is one tiny file read + a handful of JSON parses per attempt.
- The circuit-breaker only suppresses the append — it does not flip the in-process `shouldRecordCompletedBootstrapTurn` flag and does not change any other compaction / rotation / delivery decision. Other suggested mitigations in the issue (transcript size guard, media de-duplication, mark-degraded-and-skip-on-load) are deliberately left to follow-up PRs because they touch separate subsystems (`SessionManager.open`, media pipeline, session loader) and each has its own risk surface.
- Reviewed against root `AGENTS.md`, `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/CLAUDE.md` (the embedded-runner test-perf guardrails — the new tests live on the helper layer, not on the full runner), and `CONTRIBUTING.md`. No `CHANGELOG.md` edits (per CONTRIBUTING).

Refs #63998
